summary:
The Srebrenica massacre occurred in July 1995 during the [[event:3677|Bosnian War]], when Bosnian Serb forces under the command of General Ratko Mladić systematically killed more than 8,000 Bosniak Muslim men and boys from the UN-designated safe area of Srebrenica in eastern Bosnia. Over several days beginning on 11 July 1995, after Serb forces overran the enclave despite the presence of Dutch UN peacekeepers, men and boys were separated from women, transported to execution sites, and shot in mass killings. Their bodies were subsequently buried in mass graves and then exhumed and reburied in secondary graves in an effort to conceal the evidence.

The massacre was the largest act of genocide in Europe since the Holocaust. The International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia and the International Court of Justice have both legally defined the killings as genocide. The Dutch UN peacekeepers' failure to prevent the massacre and the international community's broader inability to protect the safe areas became subjects of intense scrutiny and soul-searching.

The massacre accelerated international military intervention. NATO conducted a series of airstrikes against Bosnian Serb positions in August and September 1995, combined with a Croat-Bosniak offensive, which eventually brought the parties to negotiations. The Dayton Agreement of November 1995 ended the Bosnian War.

Trials before the ICTY convicted Mladić, former Bosnian Serb president Radovan Karadžić, and other commanders of genocide and crimes against humanity. The Srebrenica massacre remains a defining event in debates about humanitarian intervention, genocide prevention, and the responsibilities of the international community.
